name.text: Week 10 – Q1: Series RLC Natural Response (Switch Opening)

questiontext.text:
Week 10 – Q1: Series RLC Natural Response (Switch Opening)

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 8 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

A series RLC circuit is powered by a DC voltage source \(V_s = {@Vs_val@}\;\text{V}\). The circuit contains a resistor \(R = {@R_val@}\;\Omega\), an inductor \(L = {@L_val@}\;\text{H}\), and a capacitor \(C = {@C_uF@}\;\mu\text{F}\), all connected in series with the source and a switch.

The circuit has been in steady state for a long time. At \(t = 0\), the switch opens, disconnecting the DC source and leaving the series RLC loop isolated.

Your task: determine the complete natural response \(i(t)\) for \(t > 0\) by working through parts (a)–(f) below.

Part (a) — DC Steady-State Concept (1 pt)

Before the switch opens (\(t < 0\)), the circuit has been in DC steady state for a very long time. Which statement correctly describes the initial conditions?

[[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]]

Part (b1) — Initial Current \(i(0^+)\) (1 pt)

Using the DC steady-state analysis and continuity conditions, what is \(i(0^+)\) in Amperes?

\(i(0^+) = \) [[input:ans2]] A [[validation:ans2]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0 or 2.5

Part (b2) — Initial Capacitor Voltage \(v_C(0^+)\) (1 pt)

What is the value of \(v_C(0^+)\) in Volts?

\(v_C(0^+) = \) [[input:ans3]] V [[validation:ans3]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 50.0

Part (c1) — Damping Coefficient \(\alpha\) (1 pt)

Calculate the damping coefficient \(\alpha\) (in rad/s) for this series RLC circuit.

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans4]] rad/s [[validation:ans4]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 15000 or 1.5e4

Part (c2) — Natural Frequency \(\omega_0\) (1 pt)

Calculate the resonant (natural) frequency \(\omega_0\) (in rad/s).

\(\omega_0 = \) [[input:ans5]] rad/s [[validation:ans5]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 3160 or 3.16e3

Part (d) — Damping Regime Classification (1 pt)

Based on your values of \(\alpha\) and \(\omega_0\), classify the damping regime:

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Part (e) — Characteristic Root \(s_1\) (1 pt)

Find the characteristic root \(s_1\) with the larger real part (or positive imaginary part if complex conjugate pair).

\(s_1 = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Syntax hint: For complex roots use j for the imaginary unit, e.g., -2800+9600*j

Part (f) — Complete Current Response \(i(t)\) (1 pt)

Write the complete expression for \(i(t)\) (in Amperes) for \(t > 0\).

\(i(t) = \) [[input:ans8]] A [[validation:ans8]]

Syntax hint: Use exp(...), sin(...), cos(...), and t as the time variable.
Example: -0.104*exp(-2800*t)*sin(9600*t)

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: DC Steady-State Analysis

After a very long time in DC steady state:

  • The capacitor acts as an open circuit (it blocks DC current after fully charging).
  • The inductor acts as a short circuit (zero voltage drop across it at constant current).

Since the capacitor is open, no current flows in the series loop:

\[i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\]

With no current flowing, there is no voltage drop across \(R\) or \(L\). All the source voltage appears across the capacitor:

\[v_C(0^-) = V_s = {@Vs_val@}\;\text{V}\]

By continuity of inductor current and capacitor voltage:

\[i(0^+) = i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}, \quad v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-) = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 2: Damping Parameters

For a series RLC circuit:

\[\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{{@R_val@}}{2 \times {@L_val@}} = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{rad/s}\]

\[\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{{@L_val@} \times {@C_val@}}} = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\]

Step 3: Damping Regime Classification

Comparing \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{rad/s}\) with \(\omega_0 = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\):

The circuit is {@regime_name@}.

Step 4: Initial Derivative

After the switch opens, KVL around the isolated series RLC loop gives:

\[L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri + v_C = 0\]

At \(t = 0^+\):

\[\frac{di}{dt}(0^+) = \frac{1}{L}\left(-R \cdot i(0^+) - v_C(0^+)\right) = \frac{1}{{@L_val@}}\left(-{@R_val@} \cdot 0 - {@Vs_val@}\right) = {@didt0@}\;\text{A/s}\]

Step 5: Complete Response

Applying the initial conditions \(i(0) = 0\) and \(\frac{di}{dt}(0) = {@didt0@}\;\text{A/s}\):

\[i(t) = {@ta8@}\;\text{A}, \quad t > 0\]

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)DC steady-state conceptOption 1: Capacitor = open circuit at DC
(b1)\(i(0^+)\){@ta2@} A
(b2)\(v_C(0^+)\){@ta3@} V
(c1)\(\alpha\){@ta4@} rad/s
(c2)\(\omega_0\){@ta5@} rad/s
(d)Damping regime{@regime_name@}
(e)\(s_1\){@ta7@}
(f)\(i(t)\){@ta8@} A

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — DC Steady-State Concept

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b1) — Initial Current \(i(0^+)\)

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (b2) — Initial Capacitor Voltage \(v_C(0^+)\)

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (c1) — Damping Coefficient \(\alpha\)

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (c2) — Natural Frequency \(\omega_0\)

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (d) — Damping Regime

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (e) — Characteristic Root \(s_1\)

[[feedback:prt7]]

Part (f) — Complete Response \(i(t)\)

[[feedback:prt8]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: R={@R_val@} Ω, C={@C_uF@} µF, Vs={@Vs_val@} V | α={@ta_alpha@}, ω₀={@ta_omega0@} | {@regime_name@}

questiondescription.text: Series RLC Natural Response — Derive initial conditions from DC steady state, classify damping regime, and find i(t). Week 10 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Correct! In DC steady state, the capacitor is an open circuit (blocks DC current), so \(i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\) and all source voltage appears across the capacitor: \(v_C(0^-) = V_s = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The correct answer is: Capacitor = open circuit at DC, so \(i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\) and \(v_C(0^-) = V_s\). In DC steady state: the capacitor acts as an open circuit (it fully charges and blocks DC). The inductor acts as a short circuit (zero voltage for constant current). Since the capacitor is open, no current flows: \(i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\), and \(v_C(0^-) = V_s = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\).

node.description: Check i(0+) value — NumAbsolute because answer is 0

truefeedback.text: Correct! By continuity of inductor current: \(i(0^+) = i(0^-) = {@ta2@}\;\text{A}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Since no DC current flows through the series circuit at steady state (capacitor is open), \(i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\). By continuity of inductor current, \(i(0^+) = {@ta2@}\;\text{A}\).
Incorrect. The capacitor voltage at DC steady state equals the source voltage. By continuity: \(v_C(0^+) = V_s = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\).

Incorrect. For a series RLC circuit, the damping coefficient is \(\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}\), not \(\frac{1}{2RC}\) (which applies to parallel RLC). With \(R = {@R_val@}\;\Omega\) and \(L = {@L_val@}\;\text{H}\): \(\alpha = {@ta4@}\;\text{rad/s}\).
Incorrect. The natural frequency is \(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\). With \(L = {@L_val@}\;\text{H}\) and \(C = {@C_val@}\;\text{F}\): \(\omega_0 = {@ta5@}\;\text{rad/s}\).
Incorrect. Compare: \(\alpha^2 = {@alpha_sq@}\) vs \(\omega_0^2 = {@omega0_sq@}\). Since \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\) and \(\omega_0 = {@ta_omega0@}\), the circuit is {@regime_name@}.

node.description: Numerical fallback: accept if real and imag parts within 2%

truefeedback.text: Correct! Your answer \({@ans7@}\) is numerically equivalent to \(s_1 = {@ta7@}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The characteristic root with the larger real part is \(s_1 = {@ta7@}\). Remember the characteristic equation is \(s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0\), giving \(s = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}\).

node.description: Try AlgEquiv first for exact symbolic match
Numerical sampling fallback: evaluate at 3 test points

truefeedback.text:
Correct! Your expression for \(i(t)\) matches the expected answer numerically at all test points.

Expected form: \(i(t) = {@ta8@}\;\text{A}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Remember to apply both initial conditions: \(i(0) = 0\) and \(\frac{di}{dt}(0) = {@didt0@}\;\text{A/s}\). The complete response is \(i(t) = {@ta8@}\;\text{A}\).

hint.text: Hint 1 (Intuition): In DC steady state, think about what happens to each component after an infinite amount of time. Which component prevents DC current from flowing?

Hint 2 (Formulas): Capacitor = open circuit at DC (blocks DC current). Inductor = short circuit at DC (zero voltage drop). For a series RLC circuit: \(\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}\), \(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\).

Hint 3 (Worked Step): With no current flowing before the switch opens: \(i(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}\) and \(v_C(0^-) = V_s\). By continuity these values hold at \(t = 0^+\). Then from KVL after the switch opens:

\[\frac{di}{dt}(0^+) = \frac{1}{L}\left(-R \cdot i(0^+) - v_C(0^+)\right) = \frac{-V_s}{L}\]
